learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Intervals, Two Pointers, Sorting, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine two people's calendars containing their busy meeting slots. The overlapping portions of their meetings represent the times when both are busy simultaneously.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine two people's calendars containing their busy meeting slots. The overlapping portions of their meetings represent the times when both are busy simultaneously.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use the Two Pointer technique whenever two sorted interval lists need to be compared efficiently.
learning.signals: Whenever two sorted interval lists must be compared to find common overlapping regions, think of the Two Pointer technique.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: You are given two lists of closed intervals, firstList and secondList, where each list is pairwise disjoint and sorted in ascending order. Return the intersection of these two interval lists.

examples.explanation: Each output interval represents an overlap between intervals from both lists.
There are no intervals in the second list.

constraints: Intervals inside each list do not overlap.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Compare every interval in the first list with every interval in the second list and record all intersections.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Each list is already sorted and non-overlapping. Compare one interval from each list. If they overlap, record the intersection. Then move the pointer whose interval finishes first because it cannot overlap with future intervals.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: The overlap of two intervals is [max(start1,start2), min(end1,end2)] if max(start) ≤ min(end).
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever two sorted interval lists must be compared to find common overlapping regions, think of the Two Pointer technique.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Since both lists are already sorted and non-overlapping, each interval is visited only once. Advancing the interval with the smaller ending point guarantees that no possible intersections are missed.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever two sorted interval lists must be compared to find common overlapping regions, think of the Two Pointer technique.
commonWrongPaths: Using start < end instead of start <= end.

hints: Compare only one interval from each list at a time.
Intersection starts at the larger starting point.
Intersection ends at the smaller ending point.
Move the pointer with the smaller ending value.
commonMisconceptions: Using start < end instead of start <= end.

pattern.name: Two Pointers
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever two sorted interval lists must be compared to find common overlapping regions, think of the Two Pointer technique.

approaches.title: Compare Every Pair
approaches.idea: Compare every interval in the first list with every interval in the second list and record all intersections.
algorithm: Loop through every interval in firstList.
Loop through every interval in secondList.
approaches.dryRun: Compare every interval pair until all intersections are found.

code.java: Brute force omitted because every interval pair is compared.

approaches.title: Two Pointers
approaches.idea: Traverse both sorted interval lists simultaneously using two pointers.
algorithm: Advance the interval that ends first.

approaches.title: Two Pointer Interval Traversal
approaches.idea: Maintain one pointer for each interval list. Compute the overlap using max(start1, start2) and min(end1, end2). If they overlap, store the intersection. Move the pointer whose interval finishes first because that interval cannot intersect with future intervals.
approaches.intuition: Since both lists are already sorted and non-overlapping, each interval is visited only once. Advancing the interval with the smaller ending point guarantees that no possible intersections are missed.
algorithm: If start <= end, add [start,end] to the answer.
If first interval ends first, increment i.
approaches.dryRun:
A=[0,2]
B=[1,5]
Intersection=[1,2]
Move A
A=[5,10]
B=[1,5]
Intersection=[5,5]
Move B

code.cpp: Uses two pointers to traverse both interval lists simultaneously.
code.javascript: Uses two pointers and computes overlap using max(start) and min(end).

visualization.description: Compare one interval from each list and move the one that finishes first.

interviewFollowUps: How is this different from Merge Intervals?
Can this work for three interval lists?
How would you find the union instead?
How would you find common free time?
interviewTips: Mention that sorting is already done.
Explain why max(start) and min(end) determine the overlap.
Move only the interval that ends first.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: The overlap of two intervals is [max(start1,start2), min(end1,end2)] if max(start) ≤ min(end).
cheatSheet.algorithm: Compare → Compute Overlap → Move Smaller End Pointer.

commonMistakes: Using start < end instead of start <= end.
